coredata.dc:description: This study uses waste chestnut peels to synthesize low-cost activated carbon-based catalysts through pyrolysis and calcination, investing distinct structural and compositional variations for sustianble application in manufacturing innovation and allied industries. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis indicated sharp crystalline peaks in the calcinated product, contrasting with the amorphous nature of carbon material with some partially crystalline and distorted graphitic phases of the pyrolyzed product. Fourier-transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis shows the presence of various functional groups such as O–H, C–O, C–H, CO, and CC. The analysis made through the scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) depicted a packed and agglomerated structure with different irregular particle shapes in the calcined carbon, while the sample prepared through pyrolysis exhibited a network-like structure with some interconnected voids. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis showed varying carbon content which is found to be 47.41 % in the case of the calcinated sample and 99.7 % in the product prepared through pyrolysis. The calcined carbon exhibited a band gap of 4 eV, while the pyrolyzed carbon showed a higher band gap of 4.8 eV. In addition, the catalyst prepared through the calcination process exhibited significantly higher luminance intensity than that produced through pyrolysis, while both samples showed an excitation-dependent emission property. This work presents two simple methods for synthesizing nanocarbon-based catalysts using waste lignocellulosic biomass. In addition, the optical properties of the resulting nanocarbons suggest that they can be further explored for their applications as catalysts or biosensing agents.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study uses waste chestnut peels to synthesize low-cost activated carbon-based catalysts through pyrolysis and calcination, investing distinct structural and compositional variations for sustianble application in manufacturing innovation and allied industries. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis indicated sharp crystalline peaks in the calcinated product, contrasting with the amorphous nature of carbon material with some partially crystalline and distorted graphitic phases of the pyrolyzed product. Fourier-transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis shows the presence of various functional groups such as O–H, C–O, C–H, CO, and CC. The analysis made through the scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) depicted a packed and agglomerated structure with different irregular particle shapes in the calcined carbon, while the sample prepared through pyrolysis exhibited a network-like structure with some interconnected voids. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis showed varying carbon content which is found to be 47.41 % in the case of the calcinated sample and 99.7 % in the product prepared through pyrolysis. The calcined carbon exhibited a band gap of 4 eV, while the pyrolyzed carbon showed a higher band gap of 4.8 eV. In addition, the catalyst prepared through the calcination process exhibited significantly higher luminance intensity than that produced through pyrolysis, while both samples showed an excitation-dependent emission property. This work presents two simple methods for synthesizing nanocarbon-based catalysts using waste lignocellulosic biomass. In addition, the optical properties of the resulting nanocarbons suggest that they can be further explored for their applications as catalysts or biosensing agents.
Synthesis and physiochemical characterizations of nanocarbon catalyst derived from waste chestnut peels via calcination and pyrolysis methods.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few decades, waste generation has increased exponentially worldwide, with solid waste being a major contributor to environmental pollution. Around 2.2 billion metric tons of waste were generated globally in 2020, as per the World Bank report (2022), and it is expected that by 2050, this figure will rise to 3.88 billion metric tons [1]. This rise has been attributed to rapid urbanization, industrialization, and economic growth. Also, the increase in population has led to increased consumption and production of goods, resulting in more waste [2]. This growing population is predicted to reach 10 billion by 2050, worsening the current waste problems [3]. In addition, organic solid waste such as food scraps, agricultural residues, and other biodegradable substances constitutes a significant portion of this global waste. Improper management and disposal of organic solid waste can cause environmental pollution, such as soil and water contamination. Moreover, when this waste is disposed of in landfills without proper treatment, it goes through anaerobic decomposition, producing methane gas and contributing to global warming [4]. Thus, mismanaged organic solid waste has become a threat to both the environment and human health. As a substantial volume of waste is generated, there is an increasing necessity to implement effective waste management, including initiatives focused on converting this waste into valuable products.

Lignocellulose waste (LCW) from agricultural, wood, and plant materials offers great potential for diverse value addition due to its excellent physicochemical properties [5]. Its cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin compositions make it an excellent candidate for producing various valuable bioproducts [6]. According to reports, the global annual generation of lignocellulosic waste is around 140 gigatons, less than 50 % of which is currently used [7]. Thus, effectively utilizing this massive amount of lignocellulosic biomass (LCB) not only presents an environmentally friendly approach to waste management but also offers the opportunity to make valuable additions to the circular economy. The development of different types of products, e.g., the preparation of catalysts, either as absorbent materials or nanocatalysts, and the preparation of biochar from this waste, has a promising approach [8].

Due to their unique and versatile physiochemical properties, nanomaterial-based catalysts are frequently practiced in the scientific community for a number of practical applications [9]. Developing a nanostructure-based catalyst from LCW falls under green and sustainable fabrication technology because such a nanocatalyst synthesis method can avoid producing toxic, hazardous byproducts and a cost-intensive synthesis approach. Among various potential nanocatalysts, nanocarbon-based catalysts are grabbing huge attention due to their diverse potential applications and capability to be synthesized from LCW [10,11]. Strong chemical stability, high electrical conductivity, and a large surface area are some well-known properties of nanocarbon-based catalysts [12]. Various forms of nanocarbon such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots have been successfully synthesized from LCB [13–15]. Apart from various applications such as wastewater treatment, catalysis, and energy storage, these nanocarbons also show their excellent utility in biomedical fields [16–18]. Lignocellulosic biomass-derived nanocarbons stand out among other nanomaterials in biomedical fields due to their good biocompatibility, extensive surface area enabling efficient biomolecule interactions, and superior electrical conductivity [19,20]. These properties contribute to the potential of nanocarbons to revolutionize biomedical technologies by enabling advanced biosensing, improved bioimaging techniques, tissue engineering, and innovative drug delivery systems [21–23]. For example, Liu et al., in their study, synthesized a wheat flour-derived porous carbon nanocomposite electrode modified with platinum nanoparticles for the electrocatalytic sensing of luteolin [24]. In another study by Li et al., nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were derived from different natural seeds [25]. These N-CDs showed excellent electrocatalytic performance for nitrite sensing, with a detection limit of 0.23 µM. Sang et al. developed a three-dimensional porous carbon material for glucose sensing [26]. All these studies showed that biomass-derived nanocarbon materials can be excellent catalysts for biosensing applications [27–29].

Various methods like chemical vapor deposition (CVD), microwave-assisted pyrolysis, hydrothermal treatment, carbonization pyrolysis, and calcination have been used to convert LCW into nanocarbon [9,30]. Furthermore, the selection of the synthesis methods depends on the properties of the desired nanocarbon, their application, scalability, and cost. Though the CVD method is known for yielding high-quality nanocarbon materials, it is often costly and involves complex processes [31]. Among these methods, simple pyrolysis and calcination are more favorable due to their simplicity, scalability, and ability to use diverse feedstocks [32]. Since various studies have been reported on the synthesis of nanocarbons and their composites using either pyrolysis [33–35] or calcination [36–38], for better understanding a comparative evaluation of both methods has been explored in the present study.

Therefore, the present work aims to fabricate low-cost activated carbon-based nanocatalysts from waste chestnut peels via two simple, cost-effective methods: pyrolysis and calcination. Furthermore, we have investigated the physiochemical properties in detail to understand the distinct characteristics of the synthesized activated carbon-based nanocatalysts using these two methods. Combined with the non-implementation of chemical activating agents, these two simple processes present an effective way of producing carbon-based nanocatalysts. In addition, characterization techniques like UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy were performed, suggesting that the synthesized activated carbon-based nanocatalysts can be further explored for biosensing applications.

Water chestnut peels (WCP) were collected from the local vegetable market in Varanasi, India. The collected peels were washed with tap water to eliminate mud and impurities. Subsequently, thorough washing with double-distilled water (DD-H2O) was performed to remove residual contaminants. The cleansed peels were then dried in an oven at 55 °C until completely dried. Thereafter, the dried peels were subjected to a mixer grinder to obtain a fine powder named WCPP (water chestnut peel powder), which was used to execute all experiments and analyses required during the present study. A laboratory water distillation machine has been used for the production of DD-H2O. For the analysis of peel biomass, proximate analysis was performed using a muffle furnace (Therelek), and apart from proximate analysis, a separate moisture analysis of the fine powder peels was performed using a moisture analyzer (Wensar).

To prepare the extract, 50 g of the prepared dried WCPP was dissolved in 100 ml of DD-H2O, and to obtain a homogeneous mixture, it was kept on a magnetic shaker along with a magnetic bead. Further, the mixture was placed at 100 °C on a magnetic shaker until it reduced to half its initial volume. Next, the 50 ml solution was cooled to room temperature and filtered using Whatman filter paper. Subsequently, the remaining biomass after extraction was oven-dried completely at 60 °C, named DWCP (digested water chestnut peel), and kept for further analysis.

In the prepared 50 ml extract, 25 g of WCPP was dissolved and heated on a magnetic stirrer at 60 °C until its volume was reduced to half. The obtained 25 ml mixture solution was incubated overnight. On the next day, the mixture was washed with DD-H2O, and the solid part was separated through centrifugation (REMI NEYA 16R) at 7000 rpm. Subsequently, the mixture was kept in an oven at 100 °C until the sample was completely dried. Then, it was divided into two equal parts named CAC (calcinated activated carbon) and PAC (pyrolyzed activated carbon) for final processing through calcination and pyrolysis to produce the required samples. Sample CAC underwent calcination by subjecting it to a muffle furnace at 500 °C for 30 min. Meanwhile, sample PAC underwent pyrolysis within an inert atmosphere at 500 °C for 30 min in a split tube furnace single zone. The inert atmosphere within the pyrolysis furnace was maintained using nitrogen (N2) gas. The flow rate of N2 gas was 200 ml per minute, and it was continued for a further 30 min after pyrolysis was completed.

In this study, different characterization techniques were performed to analyze the different physiochemical properties of the synthesized material. X-ray diffraction (XRD) by RIGAKU utilized a copper metal as an X-ray source to investigate the phase and structure of the powder samples. The analysis spanned a 2θ range of 10° - 90° with a scan range of 0.05°/s. Spectra through Fourier-transform infrared spectroscope (FT-IR) were recorded using a Nicolet iS5 instrument from Thermo Electron Scientific Instrument LLC to determine the different functional groups in the samples. The samples were prepared in KBr, and the transmittance spectrum was recorded across the 400 to 4,000 cm−1 range. Analysis using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) was conducted by CARL ZEISS MICROSCOPY LTD and examined the surface morphology and composition of the materials at the nanoscale. A thin layer of gold (Au) and palladium (Pd) was uniformly coated on the surface of the sample to minimize the charging effects. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis and elemental mapping were performed using the 51N000-EDS system developed by Oxford Instruments Nanoanalysis. A transmission electron microscope (TEM) was utilized to analyze a detailed microstructure of the synthesized samples using Thermofischer (TECNAI 20 G2 FEI). A Thermoscientific (GENESYS 180) spectrophotometer was used to record the UV-visible spectrum of the synthesized samples over a range of 200–800 nm. This analysis was performed to explore the band gap and optical properties of synthesized carbon materials. To understand the photoluminescence properties, the fluorescence spectra were obtained using a PTI Quanta master 400 spectrofluorometer.

Proximate analysis was done to probe the basic composition and properties of the WCP. It involves determining the findings of moisture content, ash content, volatile matter, and fixed carbon. The results of the proximate analysis are shown in Table 1 . The proximate analysis shows a moderate moisture content (8.6 %), a high proportion of volatile matter (72.4 %), a low ash content (4.6 %), and a substantial amount of fixed carbon (23 %). The good amount of fixed carbon indicates its potential as a raw material for producing activated carbon, which may be suitable for various applications.

The structural characterization of WCPP, DWCP, CAC, and PAC is performed using XRD analysis to investigate their crystalline nature and compositional properties. The XRD spectra of all four samples are presented in Fig. 1 . The XRD pattern of WCPP exhibits an intense peak around 2θ = 22° and a minor peak around 2θ = 18°, which can be associated with the amorphous and crystalline phases of the cellulose (JCPDS No. 03-0226) [39]. The prominent broad peak represents the high amorphous nature of the WCPP; this can be attributed to the presence of hemicellulose and lignin, known for their amorphous nature [40]. The peaks associated with lignin and hemicellulose also lie around the range of the peaks observed for the cellulosic content [41,42]; however, they cannot be clearly defined due to their amorphous nature. The XRD pattern of DWCP shows a major broad peak around 2θ = 22° and a minor peak around 2θ = 18°, similar to the peak observed in WCPP. In addition, a minor but sharp peak in the XRD pattern of DWCP shows an increase in crystallinity within the digested biomass. This could possibly be due to the loss of various components like hemicellulose and lignin during the extraction process. The XRD spectra of CAC show multiple sharp peaks, indicating the high crystalline nature of the sample. These sharp peaks can be attributed to different inorganic contents found in water chestnuts, including KCl (sylvite) and K2SO4 (potassium sulfate) [43]. The characteristic KCl (cubic) peaks were observed at 2θ values of 28.35°, 40.45°, 50. 19°, 58.64°, 66.38°,73.72°, which can be indexed to the (200), (220), (222), (400), (420), and (422) planes, respectively (JCPDS-00-004-0587). The peaks for K2SO4 were observed at 2θ values of 29.8°, 30.97°, 33.6°, and 34.45° which corresponds to (112), (130), (122), and (211) planes respectively (JCPDS-00-044-1414). In addition, the diffraction pattern showed peaks for carbon at 2θ values of 11.4°, and 41.2°, corresponding to the (001) and (100) planes respectively [44]. The presence of KCl (sylvite) in biomass-derived biochar has also been mentioned in previous studies [45,46]. These XRD patterns are consistent with findings from previous studies on biochar derived from various biomass sources [47,48]. Similarly, in another study, Sitepu et al. mentioned the crystalline peaks in the XRD pattern of carbon nanostructure derived from palm oil mill effluent [49]. The XRD spectra of PAC exhibit one major broad peak at 2θ = 23.2° and one minor broad peak at 2θ = 43.2°, showing the presence of amorphous carbon material [50]. The wide peak around 2θ = 15°–25° shows the predominantly amorphous carbon, while the broad and diminished diffraction peak around 2θ = 40°–45° can be attributed to the graphite structure [51,52]. Additionally, the broad peak around 2θ = 23° and 2θ = 43° corresponds to the reflection from (002) and (100) planes of graphite structure (JCPDS 00-056-0159), respectively [50,53]. However, the presence of a broad peak around the (002) and (001) planes suggests a combination of disordered, partially crystallized, and predominantly graphitic phases in the PAC [54]. These XRD patterns suggest that CAC, derived from the calcination process, retains a higher degree of crystallinity due to the formation of oxide compounds of different inorganic elements in water chestnut peels. On the other hand, PAC, derived from pyrolysis, shows predominantly amorphous carbon with some partially crystalline and graphitic phases.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the FT-IR data were used to investigate the functional groups in all four samples (WCPP, DWCP, CAC, and PAC). In Fig. 2, the transmittance peak, around 3400 cm−1 , in all four samples is associated with the O-H stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups [55]. The peak at 2932 cm−1 in WCPP and DWCP can be associated with C–H stretching vibration [56]. The peak at 1727 cm−1 in WCPP and DWCP signifies the stretching vibration of CO bonds, which can be associated with the quinone, aldehydes, ketone, and other acetyl groups [57]. The peak at 1620 cm−1 in WCPP and DWCP can be attributed to the CC stretching vibration, which can be due to the presence of alkenes and aromatic groups within the samples [58]. The peak around 1440 cm−1 in WCPP, DWCP, and CAC can be associated with the O–CH3 groups [59]. The small peaks between 1200–900 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–OH, C–O, and C–O–C bonds [60]. The peak around 640 and 750 cm−1 can also be attributed to the aromatic C–H out-of-plane deformations and aromatic rings, respectively [61,62]. However, a considerable variation can be seen in the FT-IR spectra of WCPP, DWCP CAC, and PAC. It is observed that the intensity of O-H has decreased significantly in CAC and PAC compared to WCPP and DWCP. In addition, peaks associated with C–H, CC, and CO stretching are also absent in CAC and PAC. This decline in intensity or complete loss is attributed to the thermal decomposition of functional groups during both calcination (at 500 °C) and pyrolysis processes (at 500 °C). A similar finding was reported in an earlier study, indicating the disappearance of CO and CC stretching peaks at 500 °C [63]. A study reported by B. Li et al. also suggests that the intensity of O–H, C–O, and C–H peaks decreased at high temperatures [64]. A. Y. Elnour et al. also noted a comparable outcome in their research, observing that most functional groups undergo breakdown at elevated temperatures, leading to substantial carbonization [65]. Furthermore, a marked disparity in the peaks between CAC and PAC has been identified. This distinction may arise due to the use of two different processes for carbonization. The synthesis of PAC involves an inert environment that can facilitate the breakdown of a broader spectrum of functional groups compared to CAC, leading to more substantial alterations in the composition. The absence of most bands in CAC and PAC suggests that biomass is mainly carbonized.

A scanning electron microscope was used to investigate the microscale surface morphology of the synthesized samples. The objective was also to analyze the structural alteration in WCPP induced by digestion, calcination, and pyrolysis processes. In Fig. 3 (a), the SEM micrograph represents the surface of WCPP, showing a solid and compact structure. This could be due to the fibrous composition of WCPP, which generally consists of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin; these fibers are often tightly packed and interconnected, forming a coherent matrix. The uniform surface with minimal texture variation indicates a dense cellulose matrix arrangement with minimum void spaces [66–68]. Fig. 3(b) illustrates the SEM micrograph of DWCP, where a slight distortion in the surface has been observed. This could be due to changes in biomass composition and chemical breakdowns during the extraction process [69]. Fig. 3(c) depicts the SEM images of CAC, showing an agglomerated structure with irregular shapes and sizes. Also, CAC appears more crystalline than WCPP, DWCP, and PAC, possibly due to the formation of KCl and K2SO4 during calcination, which also aligns with the XRD results. Fig. 3(d) illustrates the SEM micrograph of PAC, showing a remarkable difference compared to WCPP, DWCP, and CAC. The pyrolyzed sample shows a cage-like morphology featuring a network of interconnected voids [70]. Also, PAC appears to be more porous as compared to CAC. This structure is a consequence of thermal decomposition and the release of volatile compounds, resulting in the formation of voids [71]. Fig. 3(e–h) illustrates the EDX analysis of WCPP, WCDP, CAC, and PAC samples coupled with data on elemental weight and atomic weight percentages. In WCPP, the weight percentages of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) were determined to be 45.20 %, 7.28 %, and 47.52 %, respectively. In DWCP, the composition shifted, revealing weight percentages of 25.53 % carbon, 0.20 % nitrogen, and 40.61 % oxygen. These findings denote a considerable change in elemental composition between the raw biomass and the digested form. The observed shift in the composition can be attributed to the dissolution of water-soluble organic components during the extraction process [72]. In CAC, the weight percentages of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) were determined to be 47.41 %, 7.85 %, and 44.74 %, respectively. In PAC, an exceptional 99.74 % carbon content and a nearly negligible presence of N and O are noticed. This outcome stands out notably when compared to the findings reported by Bhat et al., where these authors reported achieving the highest carbon content of 82.8 % at 1000 °C using garlic peels [73]. This remarkable purity in the synthesized nanocarbon structure significantly enhances its catalytic activity, substantially improving its potential for different applications.

A comprehensive understanding of the structural properties of carbon materials is essential for optimizing their performance in various applications. This study employs a transmission electron microscope (TEM) to investigate the morphological and structural differences between CAC and PAC samples, and the results are presented in Fig. 4 (a–h) and Fig. 4(i–p). TEM micrographs show a notable difference between CAC and PAC. The CAC demonstrates a more ordered and crystalline microstructure compared to the PAC. The CAC particles are more uniformly shaped and sized, with slightly reduced agglomeration, as evident in Fig. 4(c). High-resolution TEM images further highlight the structural differences, showing distinct lattice fringes and sharp diffraction spots in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, as shown in Fig. 4(h). These observations confirm the higher crystallinity of CAC. The crystalline nature of CAC suggests that the calcination process effectively induces the formation of crystalline domains within the carbon matrix. This transformation is likely achieved by reorganizing carbon atoms and removing impurities [74]. Consequently, the carbon atoms can realign into a more ordered structure, enhancing the material's crystallinity [inset Fig. 4(g)]. Apart from carbon-based crystallinity, very distinct crystal planes can also be seen in [Fig. 4(g) encircled], which could be correlated to inorganic contents, based on the XRD analysis. Also, the CAC appears to have a less developed pore structure than the PAC (Fig. 4(a)). This could be due to several factors, including the collapse of pore structures or the filling of pores with inorganic residues generated during the calcination process [75]. Less porosity could affect the material's surface area and adsorption capacity, which are critical factors in applications such as catalysis and adsorption. While reduced porosity might initially appear to be a disadvantage, it can offer significant benefits in applications such as bio-sensing [76]. For example, a less porous material may improve selectivity for specific biomolecules by minimizing non-specific binding [77]. Moreover, the reduced porous structure can facilitate faster diffusion of analytes within the material, thereby enhancing the biosensor's response time [78]. These attributes make a less porous structure potentially more effective for targeted and rapid biosensing.

Compared to CAC, PAC exhibits an amorphous and disordered microstructure. TEM micrographs reveal that the particles have irregular shapes and sizes, often agglomerating to form clusters of varying dimensions, as visible in Fig. 4 (j). A defining characteristic of PAC is its more visible porosity. TEM images of PAC highlight a complex network of interconnected pores with a broad size distribution (Fig. 4(i)). The more porous nature of PAC can be suitable for applications requiring high adsorption capacities, such as in catalysis, filtration, and energy storage [79,80]. The amorphous nature of PAC is further supported by the diffuse ring pattern observed in the SAED analysis. Unlike the sharp diffraction spots in crystalline materials, this diffuse pattern indicates a lack of long-range order within the carbon matrix. The disordered arrangement of carbon atoms may contribute to the material's overall irregular and porous structure.

The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of both CAC and PAC display broad absorption bands across the region 200–400 nm, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). This is a typical characteristic of carbonaceous materials, arising from electronic transitions within the aromatic carbon framework, mainly π-π* transitions [81]. Both spectra exhibit a very similar shape, indicating a consistent carbonaceous backbone structure. However, CAC shows a slightly higher absorbance intensity throughout the spectral range compared to PAC, suggesting a higher concentration of chromophoric functional groups or a more extensive conjugated system [82]. This is also evident from the FT-IR spectrum of CAC. The absorption edge indicates the onset of significant light absorption and is crucial for estimating the optical band gap. A comparative analysis reveals that the absorption edge for PAC is blue-shifted relative to CAC, indicating a higher energy requirement for electronic transitions. This aligns with band gap values, where PAC exhibits a wider band gap of 4.8 eV compared to 4 eV for CAC. The inset of Fig. 5(a) and (b) shows the Tauc plot for the CAC and PAC [83].

CAC and PAC exhibit distinct UV–Vis absorbance spectra and band gaps, which significantly influence their suitability for various applications. The higher band gap of these materials suggests robust electronic structures, enabling reliable operation under harsh environmental conditions, including high temperatures [84,85]. This characteristic can be particularly advantageous for biosensing applications. The wide optical response range endowed by the higher band gap can facilitate the detection of a broad spectrum of biomolecules [86], making these materials suitable as channel materials in FET-based biosensors [87]. The relatively narrower band gap of CAC and higher absorbance intensity compared to PAC indicates better optical properties, which can translate to increased sensitivity and reduced electronic noise in biosensing applications [88]. The improved electronic delocalization in CAC may further facilitate electron transfer between the carbon material and biomolecules, thereby improving sensor responses. This makes CAC particularly suitable for developing highly sensitive optical biosensors for medical diagnostics, environmental monitoring [89], and other applications where detecting low concentrations of biomolecules is crucial.

The photoluminescence analysis was performed to investigate the emission characteristics of the synthesized samples PAC and CAC. The emission spectra of PAC and CAC at the excitation wavelengths of 274 nm and 350 nm have been shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b) respectively. For PAC, the excitation at the wavelength of 274 nm shows a sharp emission peak at 304 nm, suggesting a more ordered structure with fewer defects, further leading to a well-defined electronic transition state [90,91]. In contrast, when the CAC was excited at 274 nm, it showed a high-intensity broad peak around 406 nm and a small sharp peak around 304 nm (Fig. 6(b)). The broad peak in the CAC spectra indicates heterogeneous structures with various defects [92]. In addition, the consistent emission peak at 304 nm in both PAC and CAC can be associated with the π-π* transitions typically found in sp2 hybridized carbon materials [93]. Fig. 6(a) shows that when PAC was excited at a wavelength of 350 nm, the emission peak shifted from 304 to 400 nm, with an overall redshift of 96 nm. Similarly, for CAC, the small peak around 304 nm moved to 400 nm, while the broad peak around 404 nm moved to 430 nm, with a significant decrease in intensity. This excitation-dependent PL emission suggests the presence of different electronic transitions within the samples and could arise due to the localized energy levels (π-π* of CC) [94]. Furthermore, the PL intensity of CAC is observed to be almost 100 times higher than that of PAC. This high luminescence intensity in CAC could arise due to the formation of oxides of inorganic elements during the calcination process, leading to enhanced PL intensity [95]. The different luminescent behaviors of synthesized CAC and PAC can be further explored in various biomedical fields. The high-intensity broad emission spectra and tunable PL emission properties of CAC can make it suitable for biosensing and biomedical applications [96,97]. Also, the sharp PL peaks and excitation-dependent emission behaviors of PAC suggest its possible utility in multi-color bio-imaging [98].

Using water chestnut peels as a source of carbon, the preparation of low-cost activated carbon-based catalysts was performed through pyrolysis and calcination methods, resulting in distinct structural and compositional properties that were explored by various characterization techniques. The calcination method led to the formation of crystalline material, while the pyrolysis method yielded amorphous carbon material, as indicated by XRD. In addition, the SEM and TEM analysis showed that CAC had an agglomerated structure with irregular shapes, whereas PAC showed some cage-like morphology with fewer voids. The band gap for CAC was determined to be 4 eV, while PAC showed a higher band gap of 4.8 eV. The high PL intensity of CAC and the excitation-dependent PL behavior of PAC suggested their different possible applications in biomedical fields. This research opens a new way for exploring the eco-friendly synthesis of nanocarbon-based catalysts using waste lignocellulosic biomass. Additionally, the various characteristics exhibited by synthesized nanocarbons catalysts suggest their potential for further exploration in biomedical fields such as biosensing.

Basant Lal: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Asad Syed: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Abdallah M. Elgorban: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis.
